title: Unit 27 Hyper-Exhaustive Masterclass: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions

script.text: Welcome... to the Hyper-Exhaustive Grammar Masterclass for Unit 38: Impersonal Verbs. These are the verbs of 'It'. In English, we say 'It rains' or 'It seems'. In Greek, these verbs are powerful because they set the mood for the whole sentence. I'm Ava, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... why do these verbs only have one single form?

Γεια σας! These are 'A-pró-sō-pa rhée-ma-ta'. Impersonal. They don't have a person (I, you, he) because the 'Subject' is the situation itself, or the action that follows. They are always in the third person singular.
Let's start with the king of all impersonal verbs. 'Prée-pei'. Must.
Exactly. 'Prée-pei na fý-ghou-me'... it must that we leave. You don't say 'I must', 'You must' in the verb itself. 'Prée-pei' stays static, and 'Na fý-ghou-me' tells us who is doing the leaving.
It’s very efficient. 'Prée-pei na'... followed by the subjunctive. What about 'Chrei-á-ze-tai'?
'Khrei-á-ze-tai' means 'It is needed'. 'Khrei-á-ze-tai pe-ris-só-te-ros khró-nos'... more time is needed. It’s passive in form but impersonal in usage.
Now, let's look at the weather. In English, we use 'It'. In Greek, the verb just stands alone.
Yes! 'Vré-khei'... it rains. 'Khio-née-zei'... it snows. 'Phi-sá-ei'... it blows (it's windy). You don't need a subject. The action is the subject.
'Faí-ne-tai' means 'It seems'. 'Faí-ne-tai ó-ti tha vré-khei'... it seems that it will rain. 'Ghée-ne-tai' means 'It is possible' or 'It can happen'. 'Dhen ghée-ne-tai!'... it’s impossible!
Pro Tip: Impersonal verbs often act as 'anchors' for the rest of the sentence. They introduce a possibility, a necessity, or a state. Athina, are there any that use the Genitive pronoun?
Yes! 'Mou a-rée-sei'. It pleases to me. In English, we say 'I like it', but in Greek, it’s impersonal: 'It is pleasing to me'. 'Sou a-rée-sei o ka-fés?'... is the coffee pleasing to you?

It’s a different perspective on reality. Instead of being the actor, you are the person the 'pleasing' is happening to. This concludes Unit 38. Master these anchors, and you'll navigate Greek sentences with ease.
Μπράβο! Σιγά σιγά όλα γίνονται. Γεια σας!
Excellent work. We’ll see you in Unit 39.
